original_abstract:
The transfer coefficient ($\alpha$) serves as a fundamental parameter in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. Current IUPAC definitions for the cathodic ($\alpha_c$) and anodic ($\alpha_a$) transfer coefficients include the total number of electrons ($n$) within the derivative of the logarithm of the current density with respect to potential. This report evaluates the practical and theoretical limitations of such definitions, noting that they strictly apply only to single elementary steps involving the simultaneous transfer of $n$ electrons.

Drawing on Marcus’s theory of electron transfer, the authors argue that the simultaneous transfer of multiple electrons is energetically unfavorable and highly improbable compared to sequential one-electron steps. Consequently, treating most multistep, multi-electron electrode reactions as single-step processes leads to significant mechanistic errors. A common fallacy in the literature involves equating experimental Tafel slopes to $\alpha n$ and attempting to derive $n$ by assuming an arbitrary value for $\alpha$ (typically 0.5), which often results in physically inconsistent interpretations of the rate-determining step.

To rectify these inconsistencies, this report proposes a revised definition of the transfer coefficient that is independent of reaction stoichiometry or mechanism. By defining $\alpha_c$ and $\alpha_a$ purely in terms of the experimental Tafel slope—specifically as $-(RT/F)(\text{d}\ln|j_c|/\text{d}E)$ and $(RT/F)(\text{d}\ln j_a/\text{d}E)$, respectively—the coefficient becomes a directly measurable quantity. This approach ensures that the transfer coefficient remains an unbiased experimental probe, allowing for more robust subsequent mechanistic modeling of complex electrochemical systems.
humanized_abstract:
The transfer coefficient (α) is one of the basic parameters of the theory of electrode kinetics. The present IUPAC recommendations for the terms cathodic transfer coefficient (α c ) and anodic transfer coefficient (α a ) include in their derivatives the total number of electrons transferred in the cathodic or anodic process, n. Their suitability and range of applicability are demonstrated. It is stated that these definitions hold only for a single electrode process and for single elementary steps with simultaneous transfer of n electrons.

According to Marcus electron transfer theory, the simultaneous transfer of a large number of electrons is energetically unfavorable and unlikely when compared to sequential one-electron processes. Therefore, a large number of multi-step, multi-electron electrode reactions are considered to occur in a single step and lead to incorrect conclusions. A common mistake is to equate the Tafel slope to $\alpha n$ and derive $n$ by assigning an arbitrary value to $\alpha$, with $\alpha = 0.5$ being the most common, which then leads to unacceptable parameters in the rate-determining step.

This paper describes three major changes to the definition of the exchange current transfer coefficient that are necessary to correct major discrepancies that undermine quantitative exploitation of electrochemical kinetic data, particularly for complex electrochemical reactions where the path of reaction does not mimic the stoichiometry of reactants and products. These changes establish the transfer coefficient in each reactant pathway as an unbiased experimental variable that can be measured independently of its use for subsequent qualitative model fitting analyses. Specifically, the transfer coefficient is defined as $\alpha_c \equiv -\left( RT \slash F \right) \left( \text{d}\ln |j_c \slash I_c| \slash \text{d}E \right)$ and $\alpha_a \equiv \left( RT \slash F \right) \left( \text{d}\ln j_a \slash I_a \slash \text{d}E \right)$ so that its value, $-\left( \text{d}\ln |j_c| \slash \text{d}E \right)$ and $\left( \text{d}\ln j_a \slash \text{d}E \right)$, respectively, is provided directly and unambiguously in terms of the experimental Tafel slope.

document.input:
The transfer coefficient ($\alpha$) serves as a fundamental parameter in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. Current IUPAC definitions for the cathodic ($\alpha_c$) and anodic ($\alpha_a$) transfer coefficients include the total number of electrons ($n$) within the derivative of the logarithm of the current density with respect to potential.
